1:
"Now the serpent was more subtil than any beast of the field which the LORD God had made. And he said unto the woman, Yea, hath God said, Ye shall not eat of every tree of the garden?" The witnesses note the subtil serpent and the first temptation — the crux of the tempter's identity. On "the serpent was more subtil than any beast of the field," interpreters note the tempter. The reformed Gill and Calvin, the puritan Henry, and the wesleyan-arminian Clarke hold that, though a real serpent (the most subtil of the beasts) was the instrument, the true tempter was Satan, the devil, who used and indwelt the serpent: Gill notes "a real serpent... but through Satan being in it, and using it"; Henry, "the tempter was the devil, in the shape and likeness of a serpent"; "that old serpent... the Devil, and Satan" (Revelation 12:9; 20:2). A second view takes Satan as appearing in the form or likeness of a serpent; a rationalist reading takes the serpent as merely a literal beast (which the expositors reject, since a beast could not so speak and reason). The dominant Christian reading: Satan the true tempter, working through a real serpent.

On "Yea, hath God said, Ye shall not eat of every tree," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the crafty question. Calvin and Keil note that the serpent opens with a crafty, insinuating question — "Yea, hath God said, Ye shall not eat of every tree of the garden?" — casting doubt on God's word and goodness; the question subtly misrepresents God's command (which forbade only one tree, 2:16-17), suggesting God grudges man the trees; the first temptation begins by undermining trust in God's word and benevolence.

On the subtlety, the puritan Henry notes Satan's policy: Henry notes that Satan's policy was to enter into talk with the woman (the weaker, and alone), to question God's word, and to insinuate doubt; the serpent's subtlety is the craft of the tempter, beginning the assault on man's innocence. The verse displays the subtil serpent and the first temptation.

The witnesses agree the substance: the subtil serpent (the instrument of Satan) opens the temptation by questioning God's command, casting doubt on his word and goodness (Gill, Calvin, Henry, Clarke, Keil). The tempter's identity bears a crux: Satan the true tempter, using and indwelling a real serpent (the dominant reading — Gill, Henry, Clarke) vs Satan in the form of a serpent vs a literal serpent alone (rejected). The verse displays the tempter crux. The verse marks the serpent: subtil. The verse displays the debate: Satan in the serpent. The verse displays the question: hath God said. The verse displays the doubt: cast on God. The temptation displays Satan, through the serpent, assaulting God's word. The verse displays the serpent: crafty. The verse displays the tempter: Satan. The verse displays the doubt: insinuated. The subtil serpent and the first temptation display the entrance of evil and a crux: the serpent — Satan's instrument — questioned God's command, "Yea, hath God said?", casting doubt on God's word and goodness; the tempter read by the dominant view as Satan using a real serpent (Gill, Henry, Clarke). The verse displays the tempting serpent, answered by the woman (vv.2-3).
2:
"And the woman said unto the serpent, We may eat of the fruit of the trees of the garden:" The very brief verse notes the woman's answer to the serpent. The grammatical voices note her reply. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the woman said unto the serpent, We may eat of the fruit of the trees of the garden" — the woman answers the serpent's question (v.1), affirming that they may eat of the trees of the garden; she begins to correct the serpent's misrepresentation, acknowledging God's permission to eat of the trees.

On "We may eat of the fruit of the trees of the garden," the witnesses note the partial truth. Gill notes that the woman rightly affirms the permission to eat of the trees (2:16); yet, by entering into parley with the serpent, she has already stepped onto dangerous ground; the conversation with the tempter is the beginning of her peril.

On the reply, the grammatical-historical note the danger of the dialogue: the woman's engaging the serpent in dialogue, rather than fleeing or refusing, exposes her to the temptation; she begins to weigh the tempter's words; though she states the permission correctly, the conversation opens the way for the serpent's further assault (vv.3-5); the danger of parleying with temptation. The verse displays the woman's answer to the serpent.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the reply — "the woman said unto the serpent, We may eat of the fruit of the trees of the garden," the woman answering the serpent, affirming the permission to eat (Keil, Gill); the woman correcting the serpent's misrepresentation, acknowledging God's bounty (Gill); the dialogue with the tempter the beginning of her peril (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the woman's reply. The verse marks the answer: to the serpent. The verse displays the permission: we may eat. The verse displays the parley: begun. The verse displays the danger: of the dialogue. The reply displays the woman engaging the tempter. The verse displays the woman: answering. The verse displays the trees: permitted. The verse displays the peril: begun. The woman's answer to the serpent displays the start of the fatal dialogue: the woman answered that they might eat of the trees of the garden — affirming God's permission, yet, by parleying with the tempter, beginning to step into peril. The verse displays the parleying woman, stating the prohibition (v.3).
3:
"But of the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the garden, God hath said, Ye shall not eat of it, neither shall ye touch it, lest ye die." The brief verse notes the woman's statement of the prohibition with an addition. The grammatical voices note her recounting of the command. Gill and Clarke note that "of the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the garden, God hath said, Ye shall not eat of it, neither shall ye touch it, lest ye die" — the woman states the prohibition concerning the forbidden tree; she recounts God's command, though with notable alterations from the original (2:17).

On "neither shall ye touch it," the witnesses note the addition. Gill and Clarke note that the woman adds "neither shall ye touch it" — words not in God's original command (2:17, which forbade only eating); whether this addition is her own gloss (adding to God's word), or a sign of her wavering, it marks a departure from the exact word of God; some see in it the beginning of her error — adding to God's command, as the serpent led her to question it.

On "lest ye die," the grammatical-historical note the weakened threat: the woman says "lest ye die" — a weaker form than God's emphatic "thou shalt surely die" (2:17); she softens the certainty of the threat; the alterations (adding "touch," weakening "surely die") show her grasp of God's word already loosening under the serpent's influence; the diminished sense of the command prepares for her fall. The verse displays the woman's statement of the prohibition with an addition.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the recounting — "of the tree... God hath said, Ye shall not eat of it, neither shall ye touch it, lest ye die," the woman stating the prohibition, with alterations (Gill, Clarke); "neither shall ye touch it," the addition not in God's command (2:17, Gill, Clarke); "lest ye die," the weakened threat, softening God's "surely die" (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the altered command. The verse marks the prohibition: stated. The verse displays the addition: touch it. The verse displays the weakening: lest ye die. The verse displays the loosening: of God's word. The recounting displays the woman's grasp of the command already slipping. The verse displays the command: altered. The verse displays the touch: added. The verse displays the threat: weakened. The woman's statement of the prohibition with an addition displays her wavering: she recounted God's command but added "neither shall ye touch it" and weakened "surely die" to "lest ye die" — her grasp of God's word loosening under the serpent's influence, preparing for the serpent's bold denial. The verse displays the altered command, denied by the serpent (v.4).
4:
"And the serpent said unto the woman, Ye shall not surely die:" The very brief verse notes the serpent's bold denial of God's word. The grammatical voices note the contradiction. Gill and Clarke note that "the serpent said unto the woman, Ye shall not surely die" — the serpent now boldly contradicts God's word; where God said "thou shalt surely die" (2:17), the serpent flatly denies it: "Ye shall not surely die"; the temptation moves from insinuating doubt (v.1) to open denial of God's threat; the first lie, the direct contradiction of God.

On "Ye shall not surely die," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and reformed Calvin note the lie. Calvin and Clarke note that the serpent's "Ye shall not surely die" is a direct, brazen lie — the contradiction of God's express word; the serpent (Satan) is "a liar, and the father of it" (John 8:44); he denies the penalty of sin, persuading the woman that God's threat is empty; the lie undermines the fear of God's judgment.

On the denial, the grammatical-historical note the assault on God's truth: the serpent's flat denial assaults God's truthfulness; he charges God, in effect, with a false threat; the temptation now openly opposes God's word with a lie, emboldening the woman to disbelieve the consequence of sin; the denial of death is the heart of the deception. The verse displays the serpent's bold denial of God's word.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the denial — "the serpent said unto the woman, Ye shall not surely die," the serpent boldly contradicting God's word (2:17, Gill, Clarke); "Ye shall not surely die," the direct lie, denying the penalty of sin (Calvin, Clarke; John 8:44); the flat denial assaulting God's truthfulness (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the serpent's lie. The verse marks the denial: ye shall not die. The verse displays the contradiction: of God. The verse displays the lie: brazen. The verse displays the assault: on God's truth. The denial displays Satan contradicting God's word with a lie. The verse displays the serpent: lying. The verse displays the death: denied. The verse displays the deception: advanced. The serpent's bold denial of God's word displays the heart of the temptation: the serpent flatly contradicted God — "Ye shall not surely die" — the direct lie denying the penalty of sin, assaulting God's truthfulness and emboldening the woman to disbelieve the threat. The verse displays the lying serpent, promising godlike knowledge (v.5).
5:
"For God doth know that in the day ye eat thereof, then your eyes shall be opened, and ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil." The witnesses note the serpent's promise of godlike knowledge. On "God doth know that in the day ye eat thereof, then your eyes shall be opened," the grammatical voices note the insinuation against God. Gill and Ellicott note that "God doth know that in the day ye eat thereof, then your eyes shall be opened" — the serpent insinuates that God's prohibition springs from envy: God knows that the fruit will open their eyes and make them like himself, and forbids it to keep them down; the charge that God grudges man advancement; the slander of God's goodness and motive.

On "ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil," the reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Clarke note the lure. Calvin and Clarke note that the serpent promises "ye shall be as gods (or as God, Elohim), knowing good and evil" — the bait is the promise of godlike knowledge and elevation; the temptation appeals to pride and the desire to be like God, independent of him; the lure of forbidden wisdom and self-exaltation; the very sin by which Satan fell (the desire to be as God).

On the deception, the reformed JFB and grammatical-historical note the half-truth: the serpent's promise was a deceptive half-truth — their eyes were indeed opened (3:7), and they came to know good and evil (3:22), but not as gods, rather as guilty sinners who lost their innocence; the promise was fulfilled in a ruinous, not a glorious, way; the deception lay in the false hope of gain. The verse displays the serpent's promise of godlike knowledge.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the insinuation — "God doth know that in the day ye eat thereof, then your eyes shall be opened," the serpent charging God with envy, grudging man advancement (Gill, Ellicott); "ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil," the lure of godlike knowledge and self-exaltation, appealing to pride (Calvin, Clarke); the promise a deceptive half-truth, fulfilled ruinously (3:7, 22, JFB). The verse displays the serpent's promise. The verse marks the insinuation: God's envy. The verse displays the promise: eyes opened. The verse displays the lure: be as gods. The verse displays the deception: half-truth. The promise displays Satan luring man with godlike knowledge. The verse displays the God: slandered. The verse displays the gods: promised. The verse displays the knowledge: of good and evil. The serpent's promise of godlike knowledge displays the bait of the temptation: the serpent charged God with envy and promised that their eyes would be opened and they would be as gods, knowing good and evil — the lure of godlike wisdom and self-exaltation, a deceptive half-truth that appealed to pride. The verse displays the serpent's promise, taken by the woman (v.6).
6:
"And when the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was pleasant to the eyes, and a tree to be desired to make one wise, she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat, and gave also unto her husband with her; and he did eat." The witnesses note the fall — the woman and the man eating the forbidden fruit. On "the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and... pleasant to the eyes, and... to be desired to make one wise," the grammatical voices note the threefold temptation. Ellicott and Gill note that the woman, persuaded by the serpent, saw the tree as "good for food (the lust of the flesh), pleasant to the eyes (the lust of the eyes), and... to be desired to make one wise (the pride of life)" — the threefold temptation (cf. 1 John 2:16), appealing to appetite, beauty, and the desire for wisdom; the fruit, forbidden, now appears desirable on every count; the woman's desire is kindled.

On "she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat," the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the act of sin. Henry and Calvin note that "she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat" — the woman, yielding to the temptation, took and ate the forbidden fruit; the act of disobedience, the entrance of sin; the root of the fall is unbelief (disbelieving God's word and threat) and pride (desiring to be as God); the first sin is committed.

On "gave also unto her husband with her; and he did eat," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note Adam's sin: "gave also unto her husband with her; and he did eat" — the woman gave the fruit to Adam, and he ate; Adam, not deceived as the woman was (1 Timothy 2:14), yet ate, joining in the transgression; by Adam's sin, as the head of the race, sin and death entered the world (Romans 5:12); the fall of mankind. The verse displays the fall — the woman and the man eating the forbidden fruit.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the temptation — "the tree was good for food, and... pleasant to the eyes, and... to be desired to make one wise," the threefold temptation appealing to appetite, beauty, and wisdom (1 John 2:16, Ellicott, Gill); "she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat," the woman's act of disobedience, rooted in unbelief and pride (Henry, Calvin); "gave also unto her husband... and he did eat," Adam's sin, by which the race fell (Romans 5:12, Clarke). The verse displays the fall. The verse marks the tree: desirable. The verse displays the temptation: threefold. The verse displays the woman: ate. The verse displays the man: ate. The fall displays the woman and man eating the forbidden fruit. The verse displays the fruit: taken. The verse displays the sin: committed. The verse displays the fall: of man. The fall — the woman and the man eating the forbidden fruit — displays the entrance of sin: the woman saw the tree good, pleasant, and desirable, took and ate, and gave to her husband, and he ate — the threefold temptation yielded to, the disobedience committed, sin and death entering the world by Adam's transgression. The verse displays the fallen pair, whose eyes are opened (v.7).
7:
"And the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked; and they sewed fig leaves together, and made themselves aprons." The witnesses note the opened eyes, the sense of shame, and the fig-leaf covering. On "the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked," the grammatical voices note the new, guilty knowledge. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked" — their eyes were indeed opened (as the serpent promised, v.5), but not to godlike glory; they came to a guilty, shameful self-knowledge; they perceived their nakedness, now a source of shame (contrast 2:25); the loss of innocence brought the consciousness of guilt and shame.

On "they knew that they were naked," the reformed Calvin notes the shame. Calvin and Gill note that the knowledge of their nakedness is the knowledge of their lost innocence; sin brought shame; their consciences accused them; the nakedness, before without shame (2:25), now exposed their guilt; the entrance of shame is the immediate fruit of sin.

On "they sewed fig leaves together, and made themselves aprons," the grammatical-historical note the inadequate covering: "they sewed fig leaves together, and made themselves aprons" — they sought to cover their shame with fig leaves; the figure of man's vain attempt to cover his guilt by his own devices; the fig-leaf aprons, an inadequate covering, mark the futile effort of fallen man to hide his sin; only God's covering (the coats of skins, v.21) would suffice. The verse displays the opened eyes, the sense of shame, and the fig-leaf covering.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the opened eyes — "the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked," the guilty self-knowledge, not godlike glory but shame (Keil, Ellicott); "they knew that they were naked," the shame of lost innocence, the conscience accusing (Calvin, Gill); "they sewed fig leaves... made themselves aprons," the vain, inadequate covering of fallen man (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the ashamed pair. The verse marks the eyes: opened. The verse displays the knowledge: of nakedness. The verse displays the shame: entered. The verse displays the covering: fig leaves. The fall's fruit displays the pair ashamed and vainly covering their guilt. The verse displays the eyes: opened. The verse displays the shame: felt. The verse displays the leaves: sewn. The opened eyes, the sense of shame, and the fig-leaf covering display the immediate fruit of sin: their eyes were opened to their nakedness and shame, and they sewed fig leaves to cover themselves — the guilty self-knowledge of lost innocence, and the vain attempt of fallen man to cover his guilt by his own devices. The verse displays the hiding pair, who flee God's presence (v.8).
8:
"And they heard the voice of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the day: and Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God amongst the trees of the garden." The witnesses note the guilty pair hiding from God's presence. On "they heard the voice of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the day," the grammatical voices note the divine approach. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "they heard the voice (or sound) of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the day" — they heard God's approach in the garden (the sound of his presence, in the cool of the evening); God comes to the garden, to call the fallen pair to account; the once-welcome presence of God is now dreaded.

On "Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God," the reformed Calvin notes the flight of guilt. Calvin and Gill note that "Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God amongst the trees" — guilt drove them to hide from God; the folly and fear of sin, that thinks to hide from the omniscient, omnipresent God; the once-loved fellowship with God is now flight and concealment; the conscience of the guilty flees from God.

On the hiding, the grammatical-historical note the change wrought by sin: the hiding marks the alienation sin has caused — fear, shame, and estrangement have replaced the innocent fellowship with God; the fallen pair, who once delighted in God's presence, now flee it; sin separates man from God; the vain attempt to hide shows the folly and misery of the guilty conscience. The verse displays the guilty pair hiding from God's presence.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the approach — "they heard the voice of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the day," God's dreaded approach to call the fallen to account (Keil, Ellicott); "Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God," guilt driving them to hide from the omnipresent God (Calvin, Gill); the hiding marking the estrangement sin has caused (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hiding pair. The verse marks the voice: of the LORD God. The verse displays the approach: in the cool. The verse displays the hiding: from God. The verse displays the guilt: fleeing. The flight displays the guilty pair estranged from God. The verse displays the LORD: approaching. The verse displays the pair: hiding. The verse displays the fellowship: broken. The guilty pair hiding from God's presence displays the estrangement of sin: hearing God walking in the garden, Adam and his wife hid themselves from his presence — guilt driving them to flee the once-loved fellowship with God, the folly and fear of the guilty conscience that thinks to hide from the omnipresent God. The verse displays the hiding pair, called by God (v.9).
9:
"And the LORD God called unto Adam, and said unto him, Where art thou?" The very brief verse notes God's call to the hiding Adam. The grammatical voices note the searching question. Gill and Calvin note that "the LORD God called unto Adam, and said unto him, Where art thou?" — God calls to the hiding Adam; "Where art thou?" is not a question of ignorance (God knew where Adam was), but a searching, summoning call — to bring Adam out of his hiding, to convict him, and to call him to account; the question awakens conscience and invites confession.

On "the LORD God called unto Adam," the puritan Henry notes the grace in the call. Henry notes that God's calling Adam, rather than destroying him at once, is an act of grace — God seeks the fallen sinner; the call is the first step of God's gracious dealing with fallen man; God comes seeking, as he would seek the lost throughout Scripture; mercy is mingled with the judgment.

On "Where art thou?", the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the convicting summons: "Where art thou?" — the question summons Adam to consider his state: where he has brought himself by sin, fallen from innocence and fellowship into guilt and hiding; the question is meant to bring Adam to self-examination, confession, and the sense of his lost condition; God's searching word calls the sinner to account. The verse displays God's call to the hiding Adam.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the call — "the LORD God called unto Adam, and said unto him, Where art thou?" God's searching, summoning call to the hiding Adam, not of ignorance but to convict and call to account (Gill, Calvin); God's calling Adam an act of grace, seeking the fallen sinner (Henry); "Where art thou?" the convicting summons to self-examination and confession (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the divine call. The verse marks the call: to Adam. The verse displays the question: where art thou. The verse displays the grace: God seeking. The verse displays the conviction: of conscience. The call displays God seeking and summoning the fallen sinner. The verse displays the LORD: calling. The verse displays the Adam: sought. The verse displays the question: searching. God's call to the hiding Adam displays the gracious, searching summons: the LORD God called, "Where art thou?" — not of ignorance, but to bring Adam out of hiding, convict his conscience, and call him to account, the first act of God's gracious seeking of the fallen sinner. The verse displays the called Adam, who answers in fear (v.10).
10:
"And he said, I heard thy voice in the garden, and I was afraid, because I was naked; and I hid myself." The brief verse notes Adam's confession of fear and hiding. The grammatical voices note the answer. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "I heard thy voice in the garden, and I was afraid, because I was naked; and I hid myself" — Adam answers God's call, confessing that he heard God's voice, was afraid, and hid because of his nakedness; the answer betrays his guilt — the fear, shame, and hiding are the marks of a guilty conscience; the once-confident fellowship is now fear and concealment.

On "I was afraid, because I was naked," the reformed Calvin notes the fear of guilt. Calvin and Gill note that Adam's fear and sense of nakedness are the fruit of sin — the guilty conscience dreads God's presence; the nakedness he had not noticed in innocence (2:25) now fills him with fear and shame; the fear betrays the change sin has wrought; the sinner dreads the God he once loved.

On "I hid myself," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the confession of flight: "I hid myself" — Adam admits his hiding; yet his answer, while confessing fear and hiding, does not yet confess the sin itself (the eating); he speaks of the symptom (shame, fear) but not the cause (disobedience); the partial confession of the guilty, who feels the effect of sin but is slow to own the sin. The verse displays Adam's confession of fear and hiding.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the answer — "I heard thy voice... and I was afraid, because I was naked; and I hid myself," Adam confessing fear, shame, and hiding, the marks of a guilty conscience (Keil, Gill); "I was afraid, because I was naked," the fear of guilt, the sinner dreading God (Calvin, Gill); "I hid myself," the confession of flight, yet not yet of the sin itself (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fearful Adam. The verse marks the hearing: God's voice. The verse displays the fear: of God. The verse displays the shame: of nakedness. The verse displays the hiding: confessed. The answer displays Adam betraying his guilt by fear and hiding. The verse displays the Adam: afraid. The verse displays the nakedness: feared. The verse displays the hiding: owned. Adam's confession of fear and hiding displays the marks of guilt: Adam answered that he heard God's voice, was afraid because he was naked, and hid himself — the fear, shame, and hiding betraying his guilty conscience, though he confesses the effect of sin and not yet the sin itself. The verse displays the fearful Adam, questioned about the tree (v.11).
11:
"And he said, Who told thee that thou wast naked? Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou shouldest not eat?" The witnesses note God's probing question about the tree. On "Who told thee that thou wast naked?", the grammatical voices note the searching inquiry. Ellicott and Gill note that "Who told thee that thou wast naked?" — God presses Adam further; the sense of nakedness and shame is not natural but the fruit of guilt; the question draws Adam toward the real issue — how he came to this shameful self-knowledge; God leads him to confront the cause.

On "Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou shouldest not eat?", the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the direct charge. Henry and Calvin note that God asks directly, "Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou shouldest not eat?" — God brings the matter to a point: the transgression of his express command; the question is meant to elicit confession; God names the sin (eating of the forbidden tree, against his command), pressing Adam to own his disobedience.

On the inquiry, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the call to confession: God's questions (vv.9, 11) are designed to bring Adam to confession and repentance; God deals with the sinner not by immediate destruction but by inquiry, that he may acknowledge his sin; the gracious method of conviction, leading the guilty to own his transgression. The verse displays God's probing question about the tree.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inquiry — "Who told thee that thou wast naked?", God pressing Adam toward the cause of his shame (Ellicott, Gill); "Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou shouldest not eat?", God's direct charge, naming the sin to elicit confession (Henry, Calvin); the questions designed to bring Adam to confession and repentance (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the probing God. The verse marks the question: who told thee. The verse displays the charge: hast thou eaten. The verse displays the sin: named. The verse displays the purpose: confession. The inquiry displays God pressing Adam to own his sin. The verse displays the nakedness: questioned. The verse displays the tree: charged. The verse displays the confession: sought. God's probing question about the tree displays the gracious conviction of the sinner: God asked who told Adam he was naked, and whether he had eaten of the forbidden tree — pressing Adam toward the cause of his shame and naming his sin, to bring him to confession of his disobedience. The verse displays the probed Adam, who shifts the blame (v.12).
12:
"And the man said, The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat." The brief verse notes Adam blaming the woman and God. The grammatical voices note the shifting of blame. Gill and Calvin note that "The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat" — Adam, instead of frankly confessing his sin, shifts the blame: to the woman ("she gave me of the tree"), and, by implication, to God himself ("the woman whom thou gavest to be with me"); he excuses himself, accusing both his wife and his Maker; the evasion and self-justification of the guilty.

On "The woman whom thou gavest to be with me," the reformed Calvin notes the blaming of God. Calvin and Gill note that Adam's words subtly cast blame on God — "the woman whom thou gavest" — as if God, by giving him the woman, were the occasion of his sin; the ingratitude and impudence of fallen man, who blames God's good gift for his own fault; the root of self-justification that will not own its guilt.

On "she gave me of the tree, and I did eat," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the reluctant admission: Adam does finally admit "and I did eat" — yet only after shifting the blame; his confession is grudging and self-excusing; he owns the act but not the guilt; the manner of fallen man, who confesses under pressure but seeks to transfer the fault; the failure of true repentance. The verse displays Adam blaming the woman and God.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the blame-shifting — "The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat," Adam shifting the blame to the woman and, by implication, to God (Gill, Calvin); "the woman whom thou gavest," casting blame on God's gift, the ingratitude of fallen man (Calvin, Gill); "and I did eat," the grudging, self-excusing admission, owning the act but not the guilt (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the excusing Adam. The verse marks the blame: on the woman. The verse displays the charge: on God's gift. The verse displays the admission: I did eat. The verse displays the evasion: self-justifying. The answer displays Adam excusing himself and blaming others. The verse displays the woman: blamed. The verse displays the God: implicated. The verse displays the confession: grudging. Adam blaming the woman and God displays the evasion of the guilty: Adam blamed the woman God gave him, and only grudgingly admitted "I did eat" — shifting the fault to his wife and to God's good gift, the self-justification of fallen man that owns the act but not the guilt. The verse displays the excusing Adam, before the woman's answer (v.13).
13:
"And the LORD God said unto the woman, What is this that thou hast done? And the woman said, The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat." The brief verse notes God questioning the woman and her blaming the serpent. The grammatical voices note the inquiry and the answer. Gill and Calvin note that "the LORD God said unto the woman, What is this that thou hast done? And the woman said, The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat" — God turns to the woman with a searching question; and she, like Adam, shifts the blame — to the serpent ("the serpent beguiled me"); each accuses another, none frankly owning the guilt; the pattern of excuse continues.

On "What is this that thou hast done?", the witnesses note the convicting question. Gill and Wesley note that God's "What is this that thou hast done?" presses the woman to consider the gravity of her deed; the question is a rebuke and a call to confession; God brings her, too, to account.

On "The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the blaming of the serpent: "The serpent beguiled (deceived) me, and I did eat" — the woman blames the serpent for deceiving her; though it is true she was deceived (1 Timothy 2:14), she, like Adam, owns the act ("I did eat") only while transferring the fault; yet her statement, "the serpent beguiled me," is true and prepares for the sentence on the serpent (v.14); the guilty seek to excuse, but God will judge each in turn. The verse displays God questioning the woman and her blaming the serpent.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the inquiry — "What is this that thou hast done?", God's convicting question to the woman (Gill, Wesley); "The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat," the woman blaming the serpent, owning the act while transferring the fault (Gill, Calvin); her statement true (deceived, 1 Timothy 2:14) and preparing for the serpent's sentence (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the questioned woman. The verse marks the question: what hast thou done. The verse displays the answer: the serpent beguiled me. The verse displays the blame: on the serpent. The verse displays the admission: I did eat. The inquiry displays the woman blaming the serpent for her sin. The verse displays the woman: questioned. The verse displays the serpent: blamed. The verse displays the deed: owned. God questioning the woman and her blaming the serpent display the continued pattern of excuse: God asked the woman what she had done, and she blamed the serpent that beguiled her — owning the act while transferring the fault, yet her true word preparing for God's sentence on the serpent. The verse displays the excusing woman, before the curse on the serpent (v.14).
14:
"And the LORD God said unto the serpent, Because thou hast done this, thou art cursed above all cattle, and above every beast of the field; upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt thou eat all the days of thy life:" The witnesses note the curse on the serpent. On "Because thou hast done this, thou art cursed above all cattle, and above every beast of the field," the grammatical voices note the sentence on the serpent. Gill and Clarke note that "thou art cursed above all cattle, and above every beast of the field" — God, without questioning the serpent (the tempter, who is not invited to confess but condemned), pronounces the curse: the serpent is cursed above all the creatures; the instrument of the temptation is judged; the curse marks the degradation and abhorrence of the serpent.

On "upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt thou eat," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the degradation. Henry and Calvin note that "upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt thou eat all the days of thy life" — the serpent is condemned to crawl on its belly and eat dust; whether marking a change in the serpent's form, or (more likely) using the serpent's natural mode as the perpetual sign of its degradation and humiliation; the creature is made a token of abasement and loathing.

On the deeper sentence on Satan, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the figure: beneath the curse on the literal serpent lies the sentence on Satan, the true tempter; the serpent's crawling and dust-eating figure Satan's degradation, defeat, and abasement; the curse on the serpent is the prelude to the promise of his ultimate overthrow (v.15); the literal and the spiritual sentence are joined. The verse displays the curse on the serpent.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the curse — "Because thou hast done this, thou art cursed above all cattle," the serpent condemned without inquiry, the tempter judged (Gill, Clarke); "upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt thou eat," the degradation and humiliation of the serpent (Henry, Calvin); beneath it the sentence on Satan, figured in the serpent's abasement (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the cursed serpent. The verse marks the curse: above all cattle. The verse displays the degradation: the belly. The verse displays the abasement: eating dust. The verse displays the figure: Satan's defeat. The sentence displays the serpent cursed and degraded. The verse displays the serpent: cursed. The verse displays the belly: crawling. The verse displays the Satan: abased. The curse on the serpent displays the judgment of the tempter: God cursed the serpent above all creatures, condemning it to crawl on its belly and eat dust — the instrument of the temptation degraded, and, beneath the figure, Satan the true tempter sentenced to abasement and defeat. The verse displays the cursed serpent, with the promise of the seed (v.15).
15:
"And I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed; it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel." The witnesses note the protevangelium — the seed of the woman bruising the serpent's head, the crux of the first gospel promise. On "I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed," the grammatical voices note the enmity. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed" — God sets a perpetual enmity between the serpent (Satan) and the woman, and between their respective "seeds" — the seed of the serpent (the wicked, the children of the devil) and the seed of the woman (the godly, and supremely Christ); the conflict of the two kingdoms through history.

On "it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel," interpreters expound the protevangelium. The reformed Gill and Calvin, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke, and Wesley read this as the first gospel promise (the protevangelium): the "seed of the woman" is, supremely, Christ — born of the woman (the virgin), who would bruise (crush) the serpent's head, destroying Satan's power and lordship by his death and resurrection (Romans 16:20; Hebrews 2:14; 1 John 3:8); "thou shalt bruise his heel" is the suffering Christ would endure (the wounding of the cross), a heel-wound, not a mortal head-wound; the singular "it/He" (the seed) points to the one Redeemer. A collective reading takes the "seed of the woman" as mankind generally, in perpetual conflict with the serpent's seed; but the Christian tradition reads it Messianically, fulfilled in Christ.

On the textual note, the grammatical-historical note the reading: the Hebrew "hu" ("it/He") and the versions (the Vulgate's "ipsa," "she," giving a Marian reading; the better reading "ipse/He," Christ) bear on the sense; the dominant Christian reading takes the conquering seed as Christ. The verse displays the protevangelium.

The witnesses agree the substance: God sets enmity between the serpent and the woman and their seeds, and promises that the woman's seed will bruise the serpent's head while the serpent bruises his heel — the first promise of redemption (Gill, Calvin, Clarke, Wesley). The seed of the woman bears a crux: supremely Christ, the Messianic protevangelium (the Christian reading — Gill, Calvin, Clarke) vs mankind collectively in perpetual conflict with evil (the collective reading). The verse displays the protevangelium crux. The verse marks the enmity: serpent and woman. The verse displays the seed: of the woman. The verse displays the debate: Christ or mankind. The verse displays the victory: head bruised. The promise displays the seed of the woman crushing the serpent. The verse displays the seed: the Redeemer. The verse displays the head: bruised. The verse displays the heel: wounded. The protevangelium — the seed of the woman bruising the serpent's head — displays the first gospel promise and a crux: God set enmity between the serpent and the woman's seed, who would bruise the serpent's head while the serpent bruised his heel — read by the Christian tradition as the promise of Christ, the seed of the woman, crushing Satan by his death (Gill, Calvin, Clarke), and by the collective reading as mankind's conflict with evil. The verse displays the promised seed, before the woman's sorrow (v.16).
16:
"Unto the woman he said, I will greatly multiply thy sorrow and thy conception; in sorrow thou shalt bring forth children; and thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee." The witnesses note the sentence on the woman — sorrow in childbearing and subjection. On "I will greatly multiply thy sorrow and thy conception; in sorrow thou shalt bring forth children," the grammatical voices note the sorrow of childbearing. Ellicott and Gill note that "I will greatly multiply thy sorrow and thy conception; in sorrow thou shalt bring forth children" — the woman's sentence is the multiplied sorrow and pain of conception and childbirth; the pains of childbearing are the consequence of sin upon the woman; what should have been pure joy is now mingled with sorrow and labor.

On the sorrow, the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the chastening. Henry and Calvin note that the multiplied sorrow is the woman's chastening for her part in the fall; yet it is mingled with mercy (the blessing of children, and the hope of the promised seed, v.15, through childbearing); the sentence is both judgment and, through it, the way of the promise.

On "thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the subjection: "thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee" — the woman is placed under the rule and headship of her husband; whether the loving subordination established at creation now made harder by sin, or a new subjection consequent on the fall; the woman's desire toward, and dependence on, her husband, and his rule over her, mark the disordered relation that sin introduced; the headship of the husband is part of the woman's sentence. The verse displays the sentence on the woman.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sorrow — "I will greatly multiply thy sorrow... in sorrow thou shalt bring forth children," the pain of childbearing the consequence of sin (Ellicott, Gill); the sorrow a chastening, mingled with mercy (the hope of the seed, v.15, Henry, Calvin); "thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee," the woman's subjection to her husband's rule (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the woman's sentence. The verse marks the sorrow: in childbearing. The verse displays the chastening: for her sin. The verse displays the desire: to her husband. The verse displays the rule: his over her. The sentence displays the woman judged in childbearing and subjection. The verse displays the sorrow: multiplied. The verse displays the children: in pain. The verse displays the subjection: appointed. The sentence on the woman displays the consequence of her sin: God multiplied her sorrow in childbearing and placed her under her husband's rule — the pains of childbirth and the subjection to her husband the woman's chastening, yet through childbearing the way of the promised seed. The verse displays the sentenced woman, before the sentence on Adam (v.17).
17:
"And unto Adam he said, Because thou hast hearkened unto the voice of thy wife, and hast eaten of the tree, of which I commanded thee, saying, Thou shalt not eat of it: cursed is the ground for thy sake; in sorrow shalt thou eat of it all the days of thy life;" The witnesses note the sentence on Adam and the curse on the ground. On "Because thou hast hearkened unto the voice of thy wife, and hast eaten of the tree," the grammatical voices note the ground of the sentence. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Because thou hast hearkened unto the voice of thy wife, and hast eaten of the tree, of which I commanded thee... Thou shalt not eat of it" — Adam's sin is named: he listened to his wife rather than to God, and ate the forbidden fruit; his disobedience to God's express command is the ground of his sentence; he preferred his wife's voice to God's.

On "cursed is the ground for thy sake," the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the curse on the ground. Henry and Calvin note that "cursed is the ground for thy sake" — the earth, on man's account, is cursed; the ground, which man tills, is brought under a curse because of his sin; the creation is subjected to vanity through man's fall (Romans 8:20); man's environment shares in the consequences of his sin.

On "in sorrow shalt thou eat of it all the days of thy life," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the toil: "in sorrow shalt thou eat of it all the days of thy life" — man's food, before freely given (1:29; 2:16), is now to be won by sorrow and toil; labor, before a happy employment (2:15), becomes painful, wearisome toil; the curse turns man's work into hardship; man shall eat his bread in sorrow all his days. The verse displays the sentence on Adam and the curse on the ground.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground — "Because thou hast hearkened unto the voice of thy wife, and hast eaten of the tree," Adam's sin of preferring his wife's voice to God's command (Keil, Gill); "cursed is the ground for thy sake," the earth cursed for man's sin (Romans 8:20, Henry, Calvin); "in sorrow shalt thou eat of it all the days of thy life," man's food won by sorrow and toil (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Adam's sentence. The verse marks the sin: hearkened to his wife. The verse displays the curse: the ground. The verse displays the toil: in sorrow. The verse displays the labor: wearisome. The sentence displays Adam judged and the ground cursed. The verse displays the Adam: sentenced. The verse displays the ground: cursed. The verse displays the toil: appointed. The sentence on Adam and the curse on the ground display the consequence of his sin: because Adam obeyed his wife rather than God and ate the forbidden fruit, the ground is cursed for his sake, and he must eat of it in sorrow all his days — his food won by toil, the creation subjected to the curse through man's fall. The verse displays the cursed ground, bearing thorns (v.18).
18:
"Thorns also and thistles shall it bring forth to thee; and thou shalt eat the herb of the field;" The brief verse notes the thorns and thistles of the cursed ground. The grammatical voices note the barren produce. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Thorns also and thistles shall it bring forth to thee" — the cursed ground (v.17) now brings forth thorns and thistles; the earth, which should yield fruit, produces weeds and useless growth; the resistance and barrenness of the ground is part of the curse; man's labor is made harder by the thorns the earth now bears.

On "Thorns also and thistles," the reformed Calvin notes the resistance. Calvin and Gill note that the thorns and thistles mark the earth's changed condition under the curse — its grudging, resistant fruitfulness; man must struggle against the weeds; the ground no longer yields its produce freely but with difficulty and against opposition; the figure of the toil and frustration of fallen labor.

On "thou shalt eat the herb of the field," the grammatical-historical note the changed food: "thou shalt eat the herb of the field" — man, who in Eden ate of the garden's trees, must now eat the herb of the field, the produce of his toilsome cultivation; his food is the grain and herbs won by labor from the cursed ground; the change from the garden's bounty to the field's hard-won produce; man eats by the sweat of his brow (v.19). The verse displays the thorns and thistles of the cursed ground.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the thorns — "Thorns also and thistles shall it bring forth to thee," the cursed ground bringing forth weeds, its resistant barrenness (Keil, Gill); the thorns marking the earth's grudging fruitfulness, man struggling against the weeds (Calvin, Gill); "thou shalt eat the herb of the field," man's food now the toilsome produce of the field (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the cursed produce. The verse marks the thorns: brought forth. The verse displays the thistles: and weeds. The verse displays the resistance: of the ground. The verse displays the food: the herb of the field. The curse displays the earth yielding thorns and man's hard-won food. The verse displays the thorns: sprung up. The verse displays the ground: resistant. The verse displays the labor: harder. The thorns and thistles of the cursed ground display the changed earth: the ground brings forth thorns and thistles, and man eats the herb of the field — the earth's resistant, grudging fruitfulness under the curse, man's food won by toil against the weeds. The verse displays the thorny ground, worked by the sweat of man (v.19).
19:
"In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread, till thou return unto the ground; for out of it wast thou taken: for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return." The witnesses note the toil of man and the return to dust. On "In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread," the grammatical voices note the labor. Ellicott and Gill note that "In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread" — man must now win his bread by hard, sweating labor; his food is the fruit of toil; the curse turns work into wearisome, exhausting labor; man eats by the sweat of his brow all his days.

On "till thou return unto the ground; for out of it wast thou taken," the reformed Calvin notes the death sentence. Calvin and Gill note that "till thou return unto the ground; for out of it wast thou taken" — man's toil continues until death, when he returns to the ground from which he was formed (2:7); death, the wages of sin, is now man's lot; the body returns to the earth whence it came; the sentence of mortality.

On "for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the mortality: "for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return" — man, formed of dust (2:7), shall return to dust; death and the dissolution of the body are pronounced; the threatened death (2:17) now takes hold; man is mortal, his body to decay and return to the earth; yet the promise of the seed (v.15) holds out hope beyond death. The verse displays the toil of man and the return to dust.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the labor — "In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread," man winning his bread by sweating toil (Ellicott, Gill); "till thou return unto the ground; for out of it wast thou taken," the toil until death, man returning to the ground (2:7, Calvin, Gill); "for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return," man's mortality, the body returning to dust (2:17, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the toiling, mortal man. The verse marks the sweat: of his face. The verse displays the bread: by toil. The verse displays the return: to the ground. The verse displays the dust: to dust. The sentence displays man laboring until he returns to dust. The verse displays the toil: wearisome. The verse displays the death: appointed. The verse displays the dust: man's end. The toil of man and the return to dust display the full sentence on Adam: man shall eat bread by the sweat of his face till he returns to the ground, for dust he is and to dust he shall return — the wearisome toil of fallen labor and the mortality of man, the threatened death taking hold, yet under the hope of the promised seed. The verse displays the mortal man, who names his wife Eve (v.20).
20:
"And Adam called his wife's name Eve; because she was the mother of all living." The brief verse notes Adam naming his wife Eve, mother of all living. The grammatical voices note the naming. Ellicott and Gill note that "Adam called his wife's name Eve; because she was the mother of all living" — Adam names his wife "Eve" (Chavvah, "life" or "living"), because she would be the mother of all living human beings; the name marks her as the ancestress of the whole race; despite the sentence of death (v.19), Adam names his wife "life," looking to the continuance of mankind through her.

On "Eve; because she was the mother of all living," the puritan Henry notes the hope. Henry and Calvin note that Adam's naming his wife "Eve" (life), just after the sentence of death, is an act of faith and hope — laying hold of the promise of the seed (v.15); through Eve, the mother of all living, would come the living seed, the Redeemer; the name expresses hope in the promise amid the sentence of death; faith looks beyond death to the life promised.

On the naming, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the significance: the naming of Eve, "mother of all living," marks the continuance of the race and the channel of the promise; though death has entered, life will continue through Eve, and through her line the promised seed; Adam's faith grasps the hope of life amid the curse. The verse displays Adam naming his wife Eve, mother of all living.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the naming — "Adam called his wife's name Eve; because she was the mother of all living," Adam naming his wife "life," the ancestress of the race (Ellicott, Gill); the naming an act of faith, laying hold of the promise of the seed (v.15, Henry, Calvin); the name marking the continuance of the race and the channel of the promise (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the named Eve. The verse marks the name: Eve. The verse displays the meaning: life. The verse displays the title: mother of all living. The verse displays the hope: in the promise. The naming displays Adam's faith naming his wife "life" amid death. The verse displays the Eve: named. The verse displays the life: hoped. The verse displays the seed: awaited. Adam naming his wife Eve, mother of all living, displays the hope of faith amid the sentence of death: Adam called his wife Eve, mother of all living — naming her "life" just after the sentence of death, an act of faith laying hold of the promise of the seed (v.15), looking to the continuance of the race and the coming of the living Redeemer through her. The verse displays the named Eve, clothed by God (v.21).
21:
"Unto Adam also and to his wife did the LORD God make coats of skins, and clothed them." The witnesses note God clothing the pair with coats of skins. On "the LORD God make coats of skins, and clothed them," the grammatical voices note the divine provision. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the LORD God make coats of skins, and clothed them" — God himself makes coats of skins for Adam and Eve and clothes them; in place of their inadequate fig-leaf aprons (v.7), God provides a fitting covering; the divine provision for man's shame, far better than man's own devising; God clothes the fallen pair.

On "coats of skins," the reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the significance. Henry and Calvin note that the coats of skins, requiring the death of animals, are widely seen as the first instance of death and (by implication) of sacrifice — the skins from slain beasts pointing to the covering of sin by the death of a substitute; the figure (in the typological reading) of the covering of sin by sacrifice, fulfilled in Christ (the righteousness that clothes the sinner); God's clothing of the pair is an act of mercy and a token of the way of atonement.

On "and clothed them," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the mercy: God's clothing the pair is an act of grace and care toward the fallen — he does not abandon them in their shame but provides for them; the covering of their nakedness is a token of his continuing mercy; even under the sentence, God shows kindness; the clothing prefigures the better covering of grace. The verse displays God clothing the pair with coats of skins.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the clothing — "the LORD God make coats of skins, and clothed them," God providing a fitting covering for the pair, better than their fig leaves (v.7, Keil, Gill); the coats of skins requiring the death of animals, seen as the first death/sacrifice, a figure of the covering of sin by a substitute (Henry, Calvin); God's clothing an act of mercy toward the fallen (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the clothed pair. The verse marks the coats: of skins. The verse displays the maker: God. The verse displays the covering: provided. The verse displays the figure: sacrifice. The provision displays God mercifully clothing the fallen pair. The verse displays the skins: from slain beasts. The verse displays the pair: clothed. The verse displays the mercy: shown. God clothing the pair with coats of skins displays his mercy toward the fallen: the LORD God made coats of skins and clothed Adam and Eve — providing a fitting covering for their shame, the skins of slain beasts (in the typological reading) figuring the covering of sin by sacrifice, God's act of grace amid the sentence. The verse displays the clothed pair, before their expulsion (v.22).
22:
"And the LORD God said, Behold, the man is become as one of us, to know good and evil: and now, lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever:" The witnesses note God's word that man has become as one of us, and the bar from the tree of life. On "Behold, the man is become as one of us, to know good and evil," the grammatical voices note the solemn word. Ellicott and Gill note that "Behold, the man is become as one of us, to know good and evil" — God speaks, perhaps with a holy irony, of man's having become "as one of us" in knowing good and evil; the serpent's promise (3:5, "ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil") is, in a sad sense, fulfilled — man now knows good and evil, but as a guilty sinner, having lost the good and embraced the evil; the "us" (as in 1:26) marks the divine plurality (the Trinity), or the divine counsel.

On "as one of us," the reformed Calvin notes the irony. Calvin and Gill note that the words carry a tone of solemn irony or reproach — man, who sought to be as God, has indeed gained a knowledge of good and evil, but to his ruin; the bitter fulfillment of the serpent's lie; man's exaltation is his fall.

On "lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the bar from the tree of life: "lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever" — God acts to prevent man, now fallen, from eating of the tree of life and so living forever in his sinful, miserable state; the bar from the tree of life is, in mercy as well as judgment, that man should not be immortalized in sin; it prepares for the way of redemption and a better, eternal life through the promised seed. The verse displays God's word that man has become as one of us, and the bar from the tree of life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the word — "the man is become as one of us, to know good and evil," the sad, ironic fulfillment of the serpent's promise, man knowing good and evil as a guilty sinner (Ellicott, Gill); "as one of us," the divine plurality, the words of solemn irony (Calvin, Gill); "lest he... take also of the tree of life... and live for ever," the merciful bar from the tree of life, lest man be immortalized in sin (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the barred man. The verse marks the word: as one of us. The verse displays the knowledge: good and evil. The verse displays the bar: from the tree of life. The verse displays the mercy: lest he live forever in sin. The word displays man fallen and barred from the tree of life. The verse displays the man: as one of us. The verse displays the tree: barred. The verse displays the mercy: in judgment. God's word that man has become as one of us, and the bar from the tree of life, display the close of the judgment: man, having become as one of God in knowing good and evil (to his ruin), is barred from the tree of life, lest he eat and live forever in his fallen state — the merciful judgment that prepares for redemption. The verse displays the barred man, driven from the garden (v.23).
23:
"Therefore the LORD God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, to till the ground from whence he was taken." The brief verse notes man sent forth from Eden to till the ground. The grammatical voices note the expulsion. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the LORD God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, to till the ground from whence he was taken" — God sends man out of the garden; the fallen man may no longer dwell in the paradise of God; he is sent to till the ordinary ground (the cursed earth, v.17-19), from which he was formed (2:7); the loss of Eden is the consequence of sin.

On "sent him forth from the garden of Eden," the reformed Calvin notes the loss. Calvin and Gill note that man's being "sent forth" from Eden is the loss of the place of innocence, fellowship, and blessing; the fallen man is excluded from paradise; the expulsion is both judgment (the loss of the garden) and the appointed lot of fallen man, to live and labor outside Eden.

On "to till the ground from whence he was taken," the grammatical-historical note the appointed labor: "to till the ground from whence he was taken" — man is set to till the cursed ground, from which his body was formed; his appointed work is now the toilsome cultivation of the earth (v.19); man returns, as it were, to the dust from which he came, to labor upon it until he returns to it in death; the life of fallen man is labor on the cursed ground. The verse displays man sent forth from Eden to till the ground.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the expulsion — "the LORD God sent him forth from the garden of Eden," God sending fallen man out of paradise, no longer to dwell there (Keil, Ellicott); the being sent forth the loss of innocence, fellowship, and blessing (Calvin, Gill); "to till the ground from whence he was taken," man set to the toilsome cultivation of the cursed earth (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the expelled man. The verse marks the sending: from Eden. The verse displays the loss: of paradise. The verse displays the labor: to till the ground. The verse displays the lot: of fallen man. The expulsion displays man cast out to labor on the cursed earth. The verse displays the man: sent forth. The verse displays the garden: lost. The verse displays the ground: tilled. Man sent forth from Eden to till the ground displays the consequence of the fall: God sent man out of the garden of Eden to till the ground from which he was taken — the loss of paradise and the appointed lot of fallen man, to labor on the cursed earth outside Eden. The verse displays the expelled man, barred by the cherubim (v.24).
24:
"So he drove out the man; and he placed at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way, to keep the way of the tree of life." The witnesses note the driving out of man and the cherubim guarding the way to the tree of life. On "he drove out the man," the grammatical voices note the expulsion. Ellicott and Gill note that "he drove out the man" — beyond merely sending him forth (v.23), God drove man out, expelling him decisively from the garden; the stronger word marks the finality and force of the expulsion; fallen man is removed from paradise and barred from return.

On "he placed at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the guard. Clarke and Gill note that God "placed at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way" — God set the cherubim (the angelic guardians) and a flaming, whirling sword at the east of the garden; the cherubim and sword bar the way to the garden and the tree of life; the figure of the angelic guard and the unapproachable holiness barring fallen man from paradise.

On "to keep the way of the tree of life," the grammatical-historical note the purpose: "to keep (guard) the way of the tree of life" — the cherubim and sword guard the way to the tree of life, that fallen man may not return and eat (v.22); the access to the tree of life is barred; the way to life is closed to fallen man by his own works, but (in the larger redemptive story) will be reopened through the promised seed (the tree of life reappears in the New Jerusalem, Revelation 22:2, restored to the redeemed). The verse displays the driving out of man and the cherubim guarding the way to the tree of life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the expulsion — "he drove out the man," God decisively expelling fallen man from the garden (Ellicott, Gill); "he placed... Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way," the angelic guard and whirling sword barring the way (Clarke, Gill); "to keep the way of the tree of life," the access to the tree of life barred, to be reopened through redemption (Revelation 22:2, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the guarded garden. The verse marks the driving out: of man. The verse displays the cherubim: guarding. The verse displays the sword: flaming. The verse displays the way: to the tree of life. The guard displays fallen man barred from the tree of life. The verse displays the man: driven out. The verse displays the cherubim: stationed. The verse displays the tree: guarded. The driving out of man and the cherubim guarding the way to the tree of life display the close of the account of the fall: God drove out the man and set cherubim and a flaming sword to guard the way to the tree of life — fallen man barred from paradise and the tree of life, the way to life closed by sin, yet (in the redemptive story) to be reopened through the promised seed. The verse closes the account of Eden and the fall, the way to life barred until the Redeemer (ch.4ff.).
